GRILLED SALMON WITH DILL

There are few dishes as quintessentially Scandinavian as salmon kissed over hot coals, finished with bright dill and a squeeze
of lemon. This simple preparation celebrates the fatty richness of wild Atlantic salmon, which becomes even more pronounced when
exposed to high heat. The skin crisps to crackling shards while the flesh inside remains tender and moist. The anise notes of fresh
dill cut through the oil, while lemon juice adds necessary acidity. This is the dish that appears on every summer table from Stockholm
to Copenhagen, evolved from ancient smoking traditions but perfected for the modern grill.

INGREDIENTS:
4 salmon fillets, skin-on (150g each)
4 tbsp butter, softened
2 tbsp fresh dill, finely chopped
1 tbsp fresh lemon zest
4 tbsp lemon juice
3 cloves garlic, minced
Sea salt and cracked pepper
1 tbsp olive oil
Lemon wedges for serving
Fresh dill sprigs for garnish

METHOD:
1. Make herb butter by combining softened butter, chopped dill, lemon zest, and minced garlic in a small bowl.
Season with salt and pepper. Set aside.

2. Pat salmon fillets dry with paper towels. Brush skin side lightly with olive oil. Season generously with salt and pepper.

3. Preheat grill to medium-high (about 200°C). Oil grill grates well to prevent sticking.

4. Place salmon skin-side down on grill. Do not move for 4-5 minutes—the skin should crisp and release easily.

5. Using a wide spatula, gently flip each fillet. Cook flesh side for 3-4 minutes until just cooked through. Fish should
flake slightly when pressed gently.

6. Transfer to serving plates. Top each fillet with a generous dollop of herb butter. It will melt over the hot fish.

7. Drizzle with fresh lemon juice. Garnish with dill sprigs and lemon wedges.

SERVING: Serve immediately with steamed new potatoes and a crisp cucumber salad. A chilled white wine complements beautifully.

COOKING NOTE: Salmon continues cooking slightly after removal from heat. Slightly underdone is better than overdone.
For thicker fillets (over 200g), reduce heat to medium and extend cooking time slightly.

VARIATION: Substitute dill with tarragon, chives, or cilantro for different flavor profiles.
